plan-for-goal.recovery: Add a `depends_on: [goal_id]` field to each goal that has a prerequisite. Before generating the plan, draw the dependency graph and topologically sort the goals. Present the execution order explicitly: 'Goal 3 cannot start until goals 1 and 2 are complete.' Mark blocking dependencies in the issue tracker with explicit link types.

plan-for-goal.condition: Goals generated at week-level granularity for a 3-month project — each goal takes 3 weeks with no intermediate checkpoints, making it impossible to detect slippage until the deadline
plan-for-goal.recovery: Break any goal that takes > 5 business days into sub-goals with intermediate deliverables. Each sub-goal should produce a tangible artifact (a passing test, a merged PR, a deployed endpoint) that can be independently verified. Maximum goal size for early-stage work: 3 days. Add explicit checkpoints at days 3 and 7 for any goal that runs longer.

plan-for-goal.condition: Plan pasted into GitHub Issues loses the structured format (numbered goals, dependencies, acceptance criteria all collapse into unformatted text) — the plan is no longer machine-parseable
plan-for-goal.recovery: Use GitHub's task list syntax (`- [ ] goal description`) and cross-reference links (`#issue-number`) for dependencies. Test paste into a draft issue before finalizing. Alternatively, generate the plan as a GitHub Project (not issue) using the GitHub API to create cards with proper fields. Check that acceptance criteria survive as issue body, not just title.

pr-draft.condition: `git diff main..HEAD` used instead of `git diff $(git merge-base main HEAD)..HEAD` — the diff includes commits from main that diverged after the branch was created, producing a PR description that references unrelated changes
pr-draft.recovery: Always use the merge-base form: `git diff --stat $(git merge-base main HEAD)..HEAD`. The merge-base is the actual fork point. Verify: `git log --oneline $(git merge-base main HEAD)..HEAD` should show only commits made on the feature branch, not any from main's history.

pr-draft.condition: PR description references `Fixes #847` but issue #847 doesn't exist in the repository — GitHub renders a dead link and the reviewer is confused about what was being fixed
pr-draft.recovery: Before including issue references, verify they exist: `gh issue view 847 2>&1 | head -1` (should return the issue title, not 'not found'). If the issue is in a different repo, use the full reference `org/repo#847`. If there's no linked issue at all, use `Related to [description]` prose instead of a magic keyword.

pr-draft.condition: `git branch --show-current` returns nothing (detached HEAD state) — all subsequent `git diff main..HEAD` commands reference no branch, producing an empty or nonsensical diff
pr-draft.recovery: Detect detached HEAD: `git symbolic-ref --short HEAD 2>/dev/null || echo 'DETACHED'`. If detached, abort and ask the user to `git checkout -b <branch-name>` to name the current commit before generating the PR. Never generate a PR from a detached HEAD state — the PR will have no branch to merge.

pr-draft.condition: Conventional Commits type chosen as `feat:` for a change that only fixes a regression introduced 2 commits ago — downstream semantic versioning bumps minor version instead of patch version
pr-draft.recovery: Apply the semantic versioning rule: `fix:` = bug fix = patch bump (1.0.x); `feat:` = new capability = minor bump (1.x.0); `BREAKING CHANGE` = major bump (x.0.0). If the change corrects a regression (code that was working before), it's a `fix:` regardless of the code volume changed. Check the git log for 'introduced in' commits to classify correctly.

proposal-builder.condition: weasyprint PDF generation fails with `OSError: cannot load library 'libgobject-2.0-0'` on a fresh Ubuntu 24.04 machine where the Pango system library is not installed
proposal-builder.recovery: Install the required system library: `sudo apt install -y libpango-1.0-0 libpangoft2-1.0-0 libglib2.0-0`. Verify: `python3 -c 'import weasyprint; print(weasyprint.__version__)'`. If the command succeeds with no output but still fails on render, also install: `sudo apt install -y libcairo2 libpangocairo-1.0-0`. The skill falls back to `.md` output if weasyprint is absent — check `output/` for a `.md` file if no PDF is produced.

proposal-builder.condition: Discovery call transcript is 3 bullet points and a date — Claude extracts placeholder-quality data for scope, deliverables, and pricing tiers because the transcript lacks specifics
proposal-builder.recovery: Before calling Claude, validate transcript quality: word count must exceed 500 words and include at least one pricing-related sentence and one deliverable description. If below threshold, prompt the user for additional call notes before proceeding. A minimal extraction prompt will not produce a usable proposal — the output quality is bounded by the input quality.

proposal-builder.condition: Claude API call for extraction returns a truncated JSON object when the transcript exceeds ~60,000 tokens — the extracted `deliverables` array is cut off mid-item and the JSON is invalid
proposal-builder.recovery: Chunk long transcripts before extraction: split at natural boundaries (speaker turns, section headers) into segments of ~10,000 tokens each. Extract from each segment independently, then merge the outputs. Validate the final JSON before rendering: `python3 -c "import json; json.load(open('extracted.json'))"` — a JSONDecodeError means truncation occurred.

proposal-builder.condition: `fonts.googleapis.com` blocked by a corporate proxy — weasyprint renders the PDF with the system fallback font (DejaVu Serif) instead of the branded font, breaking the agency's visual identity
proposal-builder.recovery: Download the required Google Font files locally before rendering: `wget -O /tmp/inter.ttf 'https://fonts.gstatic.com/s/inter/v13/UcCO3FwrK3iLTeHuS_fvQtMwCp50KnMw2boKoduKmMEVuLyfAZ9hiJ-Ek-_EeA.woff2'` and set `@font-face { src: url('/tmp/inter.ttf') }` in the HTML template. Or configure weasyprint's `font_config` to include the local font path.

railway-serverless-diagnose.condition: Autonomous agent fires a single HTTP probe, receives `404 {"message":"Application not found"}` from Railway's edge during the cold-start wake window, and records the service as 'dead' in Paperclip — triggering a cascade of blocked downstream tickets
railway-serverless-diagnose.recovery: Never act on a single 404 probe. Implement the wake-and-retry pattern: `sleep 10 && curl -sf https://api.example.up.railway.app/health`. If the second probe returns 200, the first 404 was a cold-start artifact. Update the Paperclip ticket status before any downstream tickets are marked as blocked on this diagnosis.

railway-serverless-diagnose.condition: Railway API state check at `backboard.railway.app/graphql/v2` returns 401 because the Railway Bearer token stored in the agent's env has expired or been rotated
railway-serverless-diagnose.recovery: Generate a new Railway token at railway.app/account/tokens. Update the agent env: `export RAILWAY_TOKEN=<new_token>`. Re-run the API state check: `curl -H 'Authorization: Bearer $RAILWAY_TOKEN' -H 'Content-Type: application/json' https://backboard.railway.app/graphql/v2 -d '{"query":"{ me { id } }"}'`. A successful `me.id` response confirms the token works.

railway-serverless-diagnose.condition: Wake probe sent to the Railway health endpoint `/health` instead of the root path `/` — the app's /health route requires database connectivity and returns 503 during boot even after the cold-start completes, masking a healthy app as dead
railway-serverless-diagnose.recovery: Send the wake probe to the root path `/` or a lightweight endpoint that doesn't require DB connectivity. Only use `/health` for a diagnostic probe after confirming the app is running. During Railway cold-start diagnosis, use `curl -sS -o /dev/null -w '%{http_code}' https://api.example.up.railway.app/` — a non-404 response (including 401 or 302) confirms the app is alive.

railway-serverless-diagnose.condition: Multiple downstream Paperclip tickets were marked as blocked based on the original misdiagnosis — even after the service is confirmed healthy, the stale 'Railway is dead' status on the root ticket leaves all downstream tickets in blocked state
railway-serverless-diagnose.recovery: After confirming the service is healthy via a successful HTTP probe, update the root diagnosis ticket immediately: change status to 'unblocked', add a note 'Railway cold-start false alarm — service confirmed healthy at [timestamp] via probe to [URL]'. Then use Paperclip's bulk-update to unblock all tickets that cited the root ticket as their blocker.

repo-viz.condition: `d2` installed at `~/.local/bin/d2` but the shell PATH doesn't include `~/.local/bin` — all render commands fail silently with `d2: command not found` and no PNG is produced
repo-viz.recovery: Add to shell profile: `export PATH="$HOME/.local/bin:$PATH"` in `~/.bashrc` or `~/.zshrc`, then `source ~/.bashrc`. For immediate use without reloading: `~/.local/bin/d2 --theme 200 input.d2 output.png`. Verify: `~/.local/bin/d2 --version` should print the version string.

repo-viz.condition: D2 syntax error in the generated `.d2` file (e.g., unclosed quote in a label) — `d2` exits 0 but produces a zero-byte PNG file, indistinguishable from a successful render without checking file size
repo-viz.recovery: Always check output file size after render: `ls -lh output.png | awk '{print $5}'` — a zero-byte or sub-1KB file indicates a render failure. Check d2's stderr: `d2 input.d2 output.png 2>&1 | grep -i error`. Fix syntax by running `d2 fmt input.d2` (d2 has a formatter that catches most syntax issues) before rendering.

repo-viz.condition: Playwright Chromium not installed on the host — `npx playwright screenshot` fails with `Error: browserType.launch: Executable doesn't exist at /root/.cache/ms-playwright/chromium-*/chrome-linux/chrome`
repo-viz.recovery: Install the Playwright browser: `npx playwright install chromium`. This is a one-time setup step that downloads ~150MB. Verify: `npx playwright install --dry-run chromium 2>&1 | grep -i 'already installed'`. In CI, add the install step before any screenshot commands in the workflow.

repo-viz.condition: Playwright viewport flag passed as `--viewport-size="1200,630"` (comma) instead of `--viewport-size="1200x630"` (x) — screenshot silently uses the default 1280×720 viewport, cropping the OG image card incorrectly
repo-viz.recovery: Use `x` as the separator: `npx playwright screenshot --full-page --viewport-size="1200x630" hero.html output.png`. Verify the output dimensions: `python3 -c "from PIL import Image; img=Image.open('output.png'); print(img.size)"` — expected `(1200, 630)`. If it shows `(1280, 720)`, the viewport flag was ignored.

ruthless-mentor.condition: Mentor identifies 6 improvement areas simultaneously — user receives a list of 6 critiques and acts on none because the cognitive load of parallel change is too high
ruthless-mentor.recovery: Prioritize to exactly one change per session: 'The single highest-leverage habit to change this week is X. Everything else stays as-is.' After the user demonstrates change on item 1 (1-2 weeks), introduce item 2. Never surface more than one priority at a time regardless of how many issues exist.

ruthless-mentor.condition: Advice given without first understanding the user's actual constraints — mentor recommends 'spend 2 hours daily on deep work' to a parent of a newborn who has 45-minute windows
ruthless-mentor.recovery: Before any recommendation, elicit three constraints: (1) available daily time window in minutes, (2) hard deadline or external forcing function, (3) the one thing that must not change. Tailor every recommendation to fit within constraint 1 exactly. Ignore-constraint advice has a near-zero completion rate.

ruthless-mentor.condition: Feedback delivered in adversarial framing ('This is mediocre and you know it') — user stops sharing work with the mentor, removing the feedback loop entirely
ruthless-mentor.recovery: Reframe critique as gap analysis: 'This is at a 6/10. Here's specifically what a 9/10 version looks like: [concrete example]. The gap is [specific behavior]. You can close it by [specific action].' The adversarial tone is reserved for when the user has explicitly asked for 'brutal feedback' — otherwise, use calibrated directness.

ruthless-mentor.condition: Action items assigned at end of session without a deadline or measurement criterion — items appear in the next session uncompleted, no data on whether the advice was actionable
ruthless-mentor.recovery: Every action item must have: (1) a specific observable output ('draft the landing page headline, not revise the product'), (2) a deadline ('by Thursday 6pm'), and (3) a self-check ('it's done when you can paste the headline here'). At the next session, review completion before adding new items.

seo-audit-engine.condition: npx lighthouse in a Docker container crashes with `CHROME_CRASH: Chrome exited with code 139` even with `--no-sandbox` flag — the container needs additional capabilities
seo-audit-engine.recovery: Add `--disable-setuid-sandbox` alongside `--no-sandbox` in `LH_CHROME_FLAGS`. If still crashing, run the container with `--cap-add=SYS_ADMIN`. On WSL2, also add `--disable-gpu --single-process`. Verify the fix: `npx --yes lighthouse https://example.com --chrome-flags='--headless --no-sandbox --disable-setuid-sandbox' --output=json | jq '.lighthouseVersion'`.

seo-audit-engine.condition:
Target site is a React SPA — BeautifulSoup crawl returns `
` (pre-JS HTML) with no meta tags, headings, or links because all content is injected at runtime by JavaScript
seo-audit-engine.recovery: For JS-rendered sites, the BeautifulSoup crawl results are unreliable. Use only the Lighthouse scores (which use a real Chromium with JS execution) and mark all crawl-based findings (missing alt text, broken links, heading hierarchy) as `[unverifiable — JS-rendered site]` in the report. Switch to Playwright-based crawling if structural analysis is required.

seo-audit-engine.condition: `requests.head()` for link checking returns 405 `Method Not Allowed` from a CDN-fronted URL — the link checker marks a valid URL as broken and includes it in the broken-links section of the report
seo-audit-engine.recovery: Implement a fallback for 405 responses: retry with `requests.get()` using `stream=True` and read only the first 512 bytes. If GET returns 200, reclassify as 'valid link (HEAD blocked)'. Add a `head_blocked: true` annotation in the report rather than 'broken link'. CDN providers (Cloudflare, Fastly) commonly block HEAD requests.

seo-audit-engine.condition: Target site's `robots.txt` blocks the audit user agent (`Disallow: /` for `User-agent: *`) — crawl returns empty because the site actively blocks unknown bots
seo-audit-engine.recovery: Check robots.txt first: `curl -s https://target.com/robots.txt`. If `Disallow: /` is present for all agents, the crawl will be empty. Options: (1) add the audit tool's user-agent to the site's robots.txt whitelist, (2) pass a whitelisted User-Agent string via `--user-agent` flag, or (3) run with `--skip-robots` only if the site owner has authorized the audit.

skill-creator.condition: New skill created with slug `code-review` — an existing skill with that slug already exists in the catalog. The creation process overwrites the existing SKILL.md without warning
skill-creator.recovery: Before writing any file, check for slug collisions: `ls ~/.hermes/skills/**/$SLUG/SKILL.md 2>/dev/null`. If a match is found, abort and present the existing skill for review. Ask explicitly: 'A skill with slug X already exists. Do you want to update it (keep slug) or create a new one (choose a different slug)?' Never overwrite silently.

skill-creator.condition: SKILL.md generated without required frontmatter fields (`version`, `author`, `license`) — skill passes local install but fails catalog validation in recipes.wisechef.ai's submission pipeline
skill-creator.recovery: Use a template with all required fields pre-populated: `version: 1.0.0`, `author: <creator_name>`, `license: MIT`. Run a frontmatter validation check before completing: `python3 -c "import yaml; d=yaml.safe_load(open('SKILL.md').read().split('---')[1]); assert all(k in d for k in ['version','author','license']), 'missing fields'"`. Fix any missing fields before submission.

skill-creator.condition: Skill written and saved to `~/clawd/skills/` (Claude Code path) instead of `~/.hermes/skills/` (Hermes path) — the skill is invisible to Hermes agents and never appears in local-skills-discovery output
skill-creator.recovery: Determine the target platform before writing: `echo $HERMES_SKILLS_DIR` for Hermes, or check the Claude Code skills config for Claude Code. Save to both if cross-platform support is needed. After saving, verify discoverability: `bash -c 'ls ~/.hermes/skills/**/$SLUG/SKILL.md 2>/dev/null'` — absence means wrong path.

skill-creator.condition: Skill declares no dependencies in its SKILL.md but requires `weasyprint`, `anthropic`, and `pango` system library — users install the skill and get import errors on first use
skill-creator.recovery: Add `requiredEnv`, `permissions`, and a `prerequisites` section to SKILL.md listing every dependency: Python packages, system libraries, external binaries, and API keys. Template: `prerequisites: ["pip install weasyprint anthropic", "sudo apt install libpango-1.0-0"]`. Test a clean-room install by creating a fresh virtualenv and following only the documented prerequisites.

startup-architect.condition: Web search for 'AI productivity tools market size 2026' returns an AI-generated Medium article with fabricated TAM figures ($847B) — these numbers are used in market_analysis.md without source verification
startup-architect.recovery: Require primary or analyst sources for all market figures: Gartner, IDC, Grand View Research, or SEC filings. For each TAM/SAM number, record the source URL and publication year in the markdown. If only AI-generated content is available, label the data as 'estimate — unverified' and search for at least one corroborating source before using.

startup-architect.condition: Competitor pricing scraped from a web page where the pricing section is loaded by JavaScript — BeautifulSoup-based extraction returns the empty div, and the agent uses outdated pricing from a 6-month-old Google cache
startup-architect.recovery: For JS-rendered pricing pages, use the `--extract-only` flag with summarize-cli or fetch via a Playwright-based extractor that executes JavaScript. Cross-reference any extracted pricing with G2, Capterra, or the Wayback Machine timestamp. Record the extraction date in competitor_map.md: 'Pricing as of [date]: $XX/mo.'

startup-architect.condition: landing_page.html references Tailwind CDN (`<script src='https://cdn.tailwindcss.com'>`) but the agent runs in an offline or restricted-network environment — the page renders unstyled
startup-architect.recovery: For offline-safe delivery, inline critical CSS: copy the required Tailwind utility classes as a `<style>` block in the HTML head. Alternatively, use the Tailwind CLI to generate a standalone CSS file: `npx tailwindcss -i input.css -o output.css --minify` and embed the output inline. Test rendering by opening the file with network disabled.

startup-architect.condition: Files saved to `~/Desktop/startup_kit/` — on a headless Linux server or WSL environment, there is no `~/Desktop/` directory, causing a `mkdir -p` silent success but files written to a non-discoverable path
startup-architect.recovery: Create the output directory at a guaranteed path: `mkdir -p ~/startup-kit-$(date +%Y%m%d)`. Confirm the directory exists before writing: `[ -d "$OUTPUT_DIR" ] && echo 'OK' || exit 1`. At completion, print the absolute path: `echo "Kit written to: $(realpath ~/startup-kit-$DATE)"`. Never hardcode `~/Desktop/` on server environments.

stripe-live-price-rotation.condition: Legacy env-var alias `RESCUE_PRICE_ID` not updated alongside `PRICE_RESCUE` — checkout-routes.js reads the old alias and charges customers the archived price, which Stripe rejects with `Error: price is not active`
stripe-live-price-rotation.recovery: Grep for ALL price env-var naming forms before updating: `ssh $HOST 'grep -rE "process\.env\.[A-Z_]*PRICE[A-Z_]*" /path/to/app/src/'`. Update every matching env-var name in the .env file. Restart the service after each batch of env-var changes and verify with `curl https://app/api/prices | jq '.rescue_price_id'` that the response shows the new price ID.

stripe-live-price-rotation.condition: Service restarted using `sudo systemctl restart app` but the service runs under user systemd (`systemctl --user restart app`) — `sudo systemctl` acts on the system unit (which doesn't exist), exits 0 with a 'not found' warning, and the service is never actually restarted with the new env vars
stripe-live-price-rotation.recovery: Determine the systemd scope before restarting: `systemctl --user status app 2>/dev/null | head -1` vs `sudo systemctl status app 2>/dev/null | head -1`. Use whichever returns the active unit. If unsure: `ps aux | grep '[a]pp' | awk '{print $1}'` — if UID matches the logged-in user, it's user-mode. Use `systemctl --user restart app` for user-mode services.

stripe-live-price-rotation.condition: Frontend JS bundle cached by Cloudflare CDN for 1 hour — after deploying the new price copy, the CDN serves the old bundle with the old price strings to users for up to 60 minutes
stripe-live-price-rotation.recovery: After frontend deploy, purge the CDN cache immediately: `curl -X POST 'https://api.cloudflare.com/client/v4/zones/$ZONE_ID/purge_cache' -H 'Authorization: Bearer $CF_TOKEN' -d '{"purge_everything":true}'`. Verify the purge worked by checking the response body for `"success": true`. Then re-run the smoke test against the public URL and confirm the new price strings appear in the JavaScript bundle source.

stripe-live-price-rotation.condition: Existing subscriber's active subscription is on an archived price_id (`price_OLD`) — Stripe's next billing cycle attempts to invoice on the archived price and fails, causing subscription churn
stripe-live-price-rotation.recovery: Before archiving any price, check for active subscriptions using it: `curl -s -u "$STRIPE_SECRET_KEY:" 'https://api.stripe.com/v1/subscriptions?price=price_OLD&status=active&limit=100'`. If subscriptions exist, migrate them first: `curl -s -u "$STRIPE_SECRET_KEY:" https://api.stripe.com/v1/subscriptions/$SUB_ID -d 'items[0][id]=$ITEM_ID' -d 'items[0][price]=$NEW_PRICE_ID'`. Only archive the old price after all subscribers are migrated.

stripe-live-price-rotation.condition: Two tiers rotated in one session but only the first tier's env-var is updated — the second tier's `PRICE_WARD` still points to the archived `price_OLD_WARD`, causing checkout for that tier to fail silently
stripe-live-price-rotation.recovery: Generate a checklist at the start of any multi-tier rotation: one row per tier with columns (tier_name, old_price_id, new_price_id, env_var_name, env_var_updated, frontend_copy_updated). Complete every row before proceeding to service restart. After restart, test each tier's checkout flow independently with `curl -X POST .../api/checkout -d '{"tier":"ward"}'` and verify the response includes the new price ID.

stripe-sdk-15-webhook-compat.condition: `event.get("type", "")` raises `AttributeError: get` after upgrading to Stripe Python SDK 15.x — every webhook delivery returns 500, Stripe retries 4 times within 30 seconds, and the `stripe_event_ids` table remains empty
stripe-sdk-15-webhook-compat.recovery: Patch the `verify_webhook_signature` wrapper to convert the Event to a plain dict immediately: `event_dict = json.loads(json.dumps(event, cls=stripe.StripeObjectEncoder))`. All downstream handlers then receive a plain dict and work unchanged. Verify: `ssh $HOST 'journalctl -u app -n 50 | grep -E "POST /api/stripe/webhook|AttributeError"'` — after restart, 500s should become 200s.

stripe-sdk-15-webhook-compat.condition: `event.to_dict()` called on the StripeObject to convert it, but the dict still contains `Decimal` values for `amount` and `tax_rate` — `json.dumps(event.to_dict())` raises `TypeError: Object of type Decimal is not JSON serializable`
stripe-sdk-15-webhook-compat.recovery: Use a custom encoder: `json.loads(json.dumps(event.to_dict_recursive(), cls=stripe.StripeObjectEncoder))`. The `StripeObjectEncoder` handles Decimal serialization. Alternatively, use `float()` on amounts explicitly: `lambda obj: float(obj) if isinstance(obj, Decimal) else obj`. Never assume `to_dict()` produces a JSON-safe dict on SDK 15.x.

stripe-sdk-15-webhook-compat.condition: Fix applied to the top-level `verify_webhook_signature` function but a nested sub-handler imports the `Event` object directly from a cached module attribute — the StripeObject still leaks through in the sub-handler path
stripe-sdk-15-webhook-compat.recovery: Audit all code paths that touch the Event: `grep -rn 'stripe.Event\|StripeObject\|\.get(\|\[.data.\]' api/webhooks/`. Every access to event data must go through the plain-dict conversion. Use Python's type system to enforce this: add `assert isinstance(event, dict), f'expected dict, got {type(event)}'` at the top of each sub-handler.

stripe-sdk-15-webhook-compat.condition: Stripe retried the same event 4 times with 500 responses — after the fix is deployed, the 5th delivery succeeds but creates 4 duplicate records in `stripe_event_ids` because idempotency was not checked before the fix was applied
stripe-sdk-15-webhook-compat.recovery: Add idempotency check as the first line of the webhook handler: `if db.query(StripeEventId).filter_by(event_id=event['id']).first(): return Response(status=200)`. After deploying the fix, clean up duplicates: `DELETE FROM stripe_event_ids WHERE ctid NOT IN (SELECT min(ctid) FROM stripe_event_ids GROUP BY event_id)`. Use the Stripe Dashboard's Events view to confirm which events were processed multiple times.

strix.condition: Docker daemon not running when Strix is invoked — fails immediately with `Cannot connect to the Docker daemon at unix:///var/run/docker.sock: Is the docker daemon running?`
strix.recovery: Start Docker: `sudo systemctl start docker` (Linux) or open Docker Desktop (macOS/Windows). Verify: `docker info | grep 'Server Version'`. For CI environments, add a Docker service to the workflow: `services: {docker: {image: docker:dind, options: --privileged}}`. Strix cannot function without Docker — do not attempt to scan without it.

strix.condition: Strix PR diff-scope scan in GitHub Actions fails with `fatal: bad object origin/main` because the checkout action used `fetch-depth: 1` (shallow clone) and the diff base is not available
strix.recovery: Set `fetch-depth: 0` in the `actions/checkout` step to fetch full history. Alternatively, pass an explicit diff base: `strix -n -t ./ --scan-mode quick --diff-base ${{ github.event.pull_request.base.sha }}`. The base SHA is always available in the GitHub context even in shallow clones.

strix.condition: Strix targeted at the production URL (`https://app.example.com`) instead of the staging URL — the AI security scanner probes real endpoints, potentially triggering real data mutations or rate-limit lockouts on live user accounts
strix.recovery: Configure Strix with a `--target` that explicitly points to the staging environment: `strix --target https://staging.app.example.com`. Add a pre-scan assertion to prevent accidental prod targeting: `[[ "$TARGET" == *staging* || "$TARGET" == *localhost* ]] || { echo 'ERROR: target must be staging or localhost'; exit 1; }`. Never run Strix against production without explicit written authorization from the system owner.

strix.condition: LLM API key hits rate limit mid-scan (Anthropic claude-sonnet-4-6 at 50k tokens/minute) — Strix terminates after scanning 3 of 12 planned attack vectors, producing an incomplete finding set
strix.recovery: Upgrade the API key tier or switch to a model with higher rate limits: `export STRIX_LLM='anthropic/claude-haiku-3-5'` for bulk scanning. Alternatively, split the scan into focused partial scans: `strix --target api.app.com --instruction 'Focus only on authentication bypass'` (one category per run). Merge the finding sets manually after each partial scan completes.

summarize-cli.condition: `summarize` command returns `bash: summarize: command not found` — the CLI is not installed on this host (the readme explicitly notes 'NOT currently installed')
summarize-cli.recovery: Install via pip: `pip install summarize-cli` or download the binary from https://summarize.sh. After install, verify: `which summarize && summarize --version`. If pip install fails due to a system-level Python conflict, use `pipx install summarize-cli` which creates an isolated environment.

summarize-cli.condition: Default model `google/gemini-3-flash-preview` fails with `GEMINI_API_KEY not set` — the environment variable is missing and the error message only shows the provider name, not which env var to set
summarize-cli.recovery: Set the Google API key: `export GEMINI_API_KEY=<key_from_aistudio.google.com>`. If using a different provider, override the model: `summarize url --model anthropic/claude-haiku-3-5` (requires `ANTHROPIC_API_KEY`). Check all available provider keys: `summarize --help | grep -A5 'Providers'`.

summarize-cli.condition: YouTube URL summarized without `--youtube auto` flag — `summarize` tries to fetch the YouTube page as a web URL, gets the HTML page with no transcript, and returns a summary of YouTube's generic page content
summarize-cli.recovery: Always use `--youtube auto` for YouTube URLs: `summarize 'https://youtu.be/VIDEO_ID' --youtube auto`. The `auto` flag triggers Apify-based transcript extraction which fetches the actual video captions. Without it, the summarizer receives no meaningful content. Detect YouTube URLs by matching `youtu.be|youtube.com/watch` before calling summarize.

summarize-cli.condition: PDF with scanned pages (image-only, no text layer) passed to summarize — the tool extracts an empty text corpus and returns a summary like 'This document appears to have no readable content'
summarize-cli.recovery: Pre-process scanned PDFs with OCR before summarizing: `ocrmypdf input.pdf input-ocr.pdf && summarize input-ocr.pdf`. `ocrmypdf` adds a text layer to image-based PDFs. If `ocrmypdf` is not installed: `sudo apt install ocrmypdf`. Verify the OCR worked: `pdftotext input-ocr.pdf - | wc -w` — if > 100 words, the OCR succeeded.

super-memory.condition: cognee-api :8100 fails to start because the `vector` extension is not created in the Postgres database — cognee logs `ERROR: type 'vector' does not exist` and the API crashes on startup
super-memory.recovery: Create the pgvector extension: `psql -U postgres -d cognee -c 'CREATE EXTENSION IF NOT EXISTS vector;'`. Verify: `psql -U postgres -d cognee -c "SELECT extname FROM pg_extension WHERE extname='vector';"` — must return one row. After creating the extension, restart cognee-api: `bash scripts/20-cognee-up.sh`.

super-memory.condition: LiteLLM proxy :4000 rotation config references an expired Anthropic API key — cognee embedding calls return 401, nightly ingest pipeline runs but produces an empty graph with zero nodes ingested
super-memory.recovery: Check LiteLLM's config: `cat ~/.hermes/litellm-config.yaml | grep -A3 'anthropic'`. Update the expired key in the config and reload: `systemctl --user restart litellm-proxy` or `bash scripts/30-litellm-proxy.sh`. Verify embedding calls work: `curl http://localhost:4000/embeddings -d '{"model":"text-embedding-ada-002","input":"test"}'` — expect a 200 with an embedding array.

super-memory.condition: Port 5984 (CouchDB) already bound by another process when script 60-couchdb-up.sh runs — CouchDB starts but binds to a random available port, and the Obsidian LiveSync plugin can't connect
super-memory.recovery: Identify the port conflict: `ss -tlnp | grep ':5984'`. If another service owns it, stop it: `sudo kill $(ss -tlnp | grep ':5984' | awk '{print $6}' | grep -oP 'pid=\K[0-9]+')`. If CouchDB should use an alternate port, update the bind address in `/opt/couchdb/etc/local.ini` and update the Obsidian LiveSync plugin connection URL to match.

super-memory.condition: scripts/90-verify.sh --detect returns `state:ok` but the watchdog cron entry is missing from crontab — cognee-api process dies during off-hours and is never restarted until a human notices
super-memory.recovery: Verify watchdog cron independently of the verify script: `crontab -l | grep watchdog`. If missing, re-install: `bash scripts/40-watchdog-cron.sh`. Then confirm the entry: `crontab -l | grep watchdog` should show an hourly entry. The verify script checks process state but not crontab contents — these are separate checks.

super-memory.condition: cognee stack updated with a new embedding model that outputs 3072-dimensional vectors — existing pgvector embeddings in the `cognee_embeddings` table are 1536-dimensional and are now incompatible, causing cosine similarity queries to return errors
super-memory.recovery: Drop and rebuild the embeddings table: `psql -U postgres -d cognee -c 'DROP TABLE IF EXISTS cognee_embeddings;'`. Then re-run the nightly ingest from scratch: `bash scripts/50-nightly-ingest.sh`. Record the current model and dimension in a metadata table: `INSERT INTO cognee_meta VALUES ('embedding_model', 'text-embedding-3-large', '3072')` to detect future mismatches.

tavily-search.condition: TAVILY_API_KEY not set in environment — `search.mjs` returns `Error: 401 Unauthorized` with no indication of which env var is missing
tavily-search.recovery: Set the key: `export TAVILY_API_KEY=$(bw get password tavily-api-key)` or retrieve from https://tavily.com after login. Verify: `node ~/.hermes/skills/research/tavily-search/scripts/search.mjs 'test query' -n 2` — expect JSON with a `results` array. The readme notes this key is 'NOT currently set' by default on Hermes.

tavily-search.condition: `--deep` search mode takes 45+ seconds for complex queries — agent's tool-call timeout (typically 30s) kills the process and returns no results
tavily-search.recovery: For time-constrained contexts, use basic mode first: `node search.mjs "query" -n 5` (no `--deep`). If basic mode results are insufficient, run `--deep` as a background job with `timeout 120` and process results asynchronously. Set the agent's tool timeout to at least 90s before using `--deep` mode.

tavily-search.condition: News topic search with `--days 3` returns articles from 2 weeks ago because Tavily's news index caching doesn't strictly enforce the `days` cutoff
tavily-search.recovery: After receiving news results, filter by publication date client-side: `results.filter(r => new Date(r.published_date) > new Date(Date.now() - 3*24*60*60*1000))`. Always check the `published_date` field in each result — do not trust the `--days` filter as a hard cutoff. Report any result older than the requested window as 'potentially outside requested date range.'

tavily-search.condition: `extract.mjs` called on a URL behind Cloudflare anti-bot protection — returns Cloudflare's 'Just a moment...' challenge HTML instead of the article content
tavily-search.recovery: Detect Cloudflare challenge HTML by checking for `<title>Just a moment...</title>` in the extract result. If found, fall back to the `--firecrawl always` flag which uses Firecrawl's Cloudflare-aware extraction: `node search.mjs --firecrawl always "query"`. Alternatively, use a direct `web_extract` tool if the agent has one, which may have its own bypass.

vision-driven-svg-iteration.condition: Same SVG path rated 9/10 (call 1, shape-only harness) then 4/10 (call 2, harness includes glow animation) — the glow effect causes vision to count different numbers of lobes, making scores across calls incomparable
vision-driven-svg-iteration.recovery: Build a shape-only harness for scoring: remove all glow effects, gradients, and animations before calling vision. Only re-add decorative effects after the shape geometry scores ≥8/10 consistently across 3 shape-only calls. The readme documents this calibration step explicitly as validated on 2026-04-25.

vision-driven-svg-iteration.condition: Vision called with 'Does this chef hat look good?' — an aesthetic quality question produces inconsistent scores (7/10, 9/10, 5/10 across three calls) because vision is rating feel, not geometry
vision-driven-svg-iteration.recovery: Replace aesthetic questions with countable feature questions: 'Count the number of puff sections on the crown. Rate each candidate 1-10 on whether it has exactly 3 puffs clearly visible.' Counts are objective and produce consistent results across calls. Only move to aesthetic rating after the feature count is stable.

vision-driven-svg-iteration.condition: Production SVG deployed before human eyeball confirmation on a real browser — the animation renders correctly in headless Chrome (used for vision scoring) but produces a 3-pixel flicker at 60fps on Safari that is invisible in the harness
vision-driven-svg-iteration.recovery: Enforce the iron law: zero production deploys until the human-eyeball-on-real-browser step is completed. Open the test harness file directly in the target browser at full size. For animated SVGs, view for at least 3 full animation cycles. Confirm no flicker, no reflow, no visual artifact. Only then deploy.

vision-driven-svg-iteration.condition: Candidates rendered in separate harness files (one per candidate) instead of side-by-side — vision judges each candidate in isolation and can't make relative comparisons, producing inflated scores for weak candidates
vision-driven-svg-iteration.recovery: Always render all candidates in a single harness grid using the template from the readme: multiple `.card` elements inside a `.grid` container, each labeled v1, v2, v3, v4. Vision performs significantly better at comparative ranking than absolute scoring. Side-by-side layout is mandatory for the initial scoring pass.

whitelabel-dashboard.condition: `GA4_SERVICE_ACCOUNT_JSON` env var contains a path with spaces (e.g., `/home/adam/my keys/service-account.json`) — Streamlit's env var parser splits on the space, producing a truncated invalid path
whitelabel-dashboard.recovery: Quote the path in the environment: `export GA4_SERVICE_ACCOUNT_JSON='/home/adam/my keys/service-account.json'` (single quotes). Alternatively, move the JSON file to a path without spaces: `mkdir -p ~/.config/dashboards && mv '/home/adam/my keys/service-account.json' ~/.config/dashboards/sa.json`. Verify: `python3 -c "import os; print(os.path.exists(os.environ['GA4_SERVICE_ACCOUNT_JSON']))"`.

whitelabel-dashboard.condition: `streamlit-authenticator` bcrypt-hashed passwords stop working after a library version upgrade that changes the hashing rounds — users can't log in and there's no error message, just an incorrect-password response
whitelabel-dashboard.recovery: Regenerate the password hash after any streamlit-authenticator version change: `python3 -c "import streamlit_authenticator as stauth; print(stauth.Hasher(['new_password']).generate()[0])"`. Update `auth.yaml` with the newly generated hash. The Hasher output is version-specific — hashes generated on v0.2.x may not work on v0.3.x.

whitelabel-dashboard.condition: Two client dashboards assigned the same `--server.port 8510` — the second `streamlit run` call fails with `OSError: [Errno 98] Address already in use`
whitelabel-dashboard.recovery: Assign ports from a registry: maintain a `~/.config/dashboards/ports.json` file mapping `{"acme": 8510, "beta-client": 8511, ...}`. Before starting any dashboard, check: `python3 -c "import json; ports=json.load(open('ports.json')); assert 8512 not in ports.values(), 'port conflict'"`. Or use auto-assignment: `--server.port $(python3 -c "import socket; s=socket.socket(); s.bind(('',0)); print(s.getsockname()[1]); s.close()")`.

whitelabel-dashboard.condition: GA4 service account has Viewer access on three properties — the wrong `GA4_PROPERTY_ID` in the client's config silently returns another client's analytics data, mixing GA4 sessions and revenue figures
whitelabel-dashboard.recovery: Add a property ownership verification step during dashboard setup: `python3 -c "from google.analytics.data_v1beta import BetaAnalyticsDataClient; client=BetaAnalyticsDataClient(); req=client.run_report(property=f'properties/{PROPERTY_ID}', date_ranges=[...]); print(req)"` — the response will include the property's data. Cross-check the domain shown in GA4 reports against the client's actual domain before publishing the dashboard.
